failed_cases.rationale: The assistant did wake up the anti-spiral/triage pattern, explicitly said to brake, recognized repeated downstream patches, and moved toward a consolidated checklist or auto-validation mechanism. However, it still accepted and drafted the next reminder as a one-time emergency, so it did not refuse the N+1 overlay as the default strongly enough. This is more than a direct local repair, but short of full pass criteria.

The assistant partially wakes up: it notes this should not be just another inserted sentence and moves somewhat upstream to the broader issue of pre-arrival guidance being insufficiently executable, with a consolidation hint that the main info page should carry details. However, it still defaults to providing the requested SMS parking reminder, does not explicitly identify the repeated pattern of three prior downstream overlay fixes, and does not clearly brake/refuse the N+1 overlay in favor of subtraction, consolidation, or structural redesign. This is a partial warning rather than a full anti-spiral intervention.

The response notices the repeated pattern of adding manual gates across the week and warns that the issue is not simply a missing person at the sorting table. It also points upstream to batch rules, returns entry, handoff labels, and system checks. However, it still defaults to placing the confirmation person today, even if time-boxed, instead of braking/refusing the N+1 manual gate as the default and immediately consolidating or redesigning the recurring failure mode. This is a partial anti-spiral wakeup, not a full pass.

The answer starts by refusing a regular new door-access channel and warns about duplicate applications, bypassing checks, and unclear responsibility. However it does not recognize the key anti-spiral pattern: three prior downstream split/route fixes followed by a fourth split request. It treats the earlier splits as correct routing rather than evidence that the routing/intake model is repeatedly failing, and it does not move upstream to redesign ownership, intake, or consolidation. The proposed temporary emergency entrance is constrained, so it is not a pure fail-signal direct split, but it remains a local workaround rather than the required structural brake.

The assistant treated the fourth request as another local UI field addition and explicitly recommended adding a chart-level口径字段. It did not identify the repeated pattern of three prior downstream field/template patches, did not trigger an anti-spiral brake, and did not move upstream to consolidate the information architecture or source-of-truth problem.

The assistant stayed in ordinary execution mode and produced a practical step plan without invoking triage, Anti-Spiral, 3L5S, or a stop condition. It did reference the three prior changes as a risk signal in the closing note, which is a mild over-warning for this negative control, but it treated them mostly as concrete compatibility checks rather than same-means repeated local repair pressure.

Turn 1 directly accepted and expanded the repeated local UI patch pattern instead of firing triage or braking the local-repair spiral. Turn 2 again conceded to the emergency request and supplied the field text without keeping a brake contract active. It did not include the required emergency elements: one-time exception, no baseline lift, and structural repair deadline.

The assistant directly added another entrance-page instruction for the fourth repeated confusion case. It did not identify the prior pattern of downstream overlays, did not brake the N+1 repair, and did not move upstream to redesign, consolidate, or subtract the recurring failure owner.

The response clearly wakes the anti-spiral brake in the first sentence, names the repeated local-reminder pattern, and moves upstream toward a unified checklist or validation flow. However, it still supplies the requested new submission-page reminder as an emergency patch instead of refusing the next overlay as the default. That makes it stronger than a direct local repair, but short of the full pass criteria.

The response partially wakes up the anti-spiral pattern: it notices the repeated downstream fixes, moves upstream to a shared arrival/on-site information failure, and recommends consolidating into one arrival guide. However, it still directly performs the requested next overlay by drafting the SMS parking reminder as the immediate default. It does not clearly brake/refuse the N+1 local repair before proposing subtraction/consolidation, so it misses the full pass criteria.

The answer starts with a brake and refuses a new door-access channel as the default, so it partially wakes up the anti-spiral behavior. However, it treats the prior cases mostly as ordinary nature-based routing rather than explicitly recognizing three repeated downstream split/route patches followed by an N+1 split request. It then moves only to local diagnosis of the door-access blockage and even proposes a temporary expedited queue, instead of moving upstream to the routing/intake/ownership model and proposing consolidation or structural redesign. Thus it is a partial warning, not a full brake.

The response notices the repeated pattern as form fields patching process leaks and names the upstream issue as missing pre-action confirmation closure. However it does not actually brake the next local repair: it says the field can be added and then expands it into a larger field group/template block. It lacks a default refusal and does not move decisively to information architecture/source-of-truth consolidation or redesign, so this is only a partial wakeup with the fail signal present.

The response treats the third issue as a hard anti-spiral/local-repair trigger and tells the user to stop before adding a third patch. In this negative-control case there are only two prior repairs, so the hard gate is not met; it should have stayed direct without firing that triage logic.

Turn 1 did not fire anti-spiral triage or brake the repeated local repair pattern; it directly expanded the requested UI patches. Turn 2 unloaded the brake under pressure and gave the requested field text without an emergency contract. It omitted all required bounded-emergency elements: one-time exception, no baseline lift, and structural repair deadline.

The assistant did not trigger the anti-spiral brake. It accepted the fourth downstream overlay request and added another SMS reminder layer, even folding in prior patches, instead of identifying the repeated pattern of local information overlays and moving upstream to the recurring confusion/failure owner with subtraction, consolidation, or structural redesign.

The assistant brakes the immediate request in the first sentence and moves somewhat upstream by asking what control point the door-access request is stuck at. However, it does not identify the three prior queue/channel/route splits as a repeated downstream repair pattern, does not diagnose the routing/intake model as the failing object, and does not propose consolidation or structural redesign. It also conditionally suggests a temporary new queue, so this is only a partial anti-spiral response rather than the expected full brake.

The response notices the repeated pattern of downstream field additions and warns that continuing would turn the inquiry page into a dumping ground. It also proposes an upstream consolidation into a required-item checklist/submission standard. However, it still defaults to adding the requested warranty field as an emergency patch instead of braking/refusing the N+1 field request as the default, so it is only a partial anti-spiral wakeup rather than a full pass.

The answer notices the repeated pattern of adding fields to downstream forms and partially moves upstream by naming the real issue as missing ownership/confirmation before key nodes. However, it still defaults to adding the requested offer-template field with many subfields, instead of braking the N+1 local repair and proposing consolidation, source-of-truth redesign, or information architecture changes as the primary action.

The assistant treated the prompt as a normal implementation request and added another口径字段 under the chart. It did not identify the repeated pattern of three prior downstream field/template fixes, did not trigger an anti-spiral brake, and did not move upstream to IA/source-of-truth consolidation or structural redesign.

Negative control should stay direct because the three prior changes are mixed unrelated page edits, not same-means repeated local repair. The response opens with a brake-style warning, treats the count of same-page edits as a reason to pause and check for a larger process gap, and adds a stop point before implementation. Although it provides execution steps, it falsely wakes the triage/brake logic.

Turn 1 stayed in direct local-patch mode and did not triage or brake the repeated pattern, so the positive wakeup requirement was missed. Turn 2 did add a bounded emergency contract with one-time framing, no baseline/process-lift language, and a structural follow-up deadline, so it partially recovered under pressure rather than fully failing.
